title: What Really Happened Behind Closed Doors at OpenAI

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I'm Kevin Roose, tech columnist at the New York Times.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I'm Casey Newton from Platformer.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And this is Hard Fork. This week, New Yorker writers Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz join us to discuss their spicy new profile of Sam Altman. And then it's time for one good thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well Casey, the talk of the town in San Francisco this week has been, well, there've been two talks of the town. One we already covered in our AI, that was the Claude 3 thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: This town conducts multiple conversations at the same time. We're amazing at multitasking.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: The other big talker this week has been this big piece in the New Yorker about Sam Altman.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yes, more than 16,000 words devoted to a question that has come up once or twice on Hard Fork, Kevin, which is: can Sam Altman be trusted?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yes, the writers on the piece are Ronan Farrow, famous for his work on the Harvey Weinstein investigation and others, and Andrew Marantz, who is a good friend of mine and a long-time writer at the New Yorker. They worked on this piece for a very long time, talked to many, many people in and around Sam's orbit, and tried to answer the question of like, who is this guy?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and also why does that matter, right? We're talking during a week where these systems have arguably experienced a step change in what they can do. And I think those kind of advances just naturally should draw more scrutiny onto the people running these companies. What do they know about who they are, how they operate, are they honest with each other? And this piece offers one of the more comprehensive portraits that we have had so far, I would say, on that question.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You know, Ronan Farrow investigating you has to be one of the scariest experiences, you know, you pick up the phone, it's like, 'Hi, it's Ronan.'

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I know. It also seems hot too, you know? That's what everyone wants, is just a really handsome man asking them a lot of questions, you know?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay, so let's bring in Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz, welcome to Hard Fork.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Thank you guys.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Great to be here.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I mean, truly long-time, first-time. And in fact, I brought receipts to that effect. I'm - this is your show, you can take or leave this in the edit, but I wanted to show what a devoted long-time fan I am of Hard Fork. I know the show well, I know you guys like merch, and I know you guys like disclosures, but you don't have any disclosure merch to my knowledge, so I had these made for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: One for each, one for you, one for you. I'm gonna put it in the mail after we get off, but...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Come on!

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: One of them says 'I work for the New York Times, which is suing OpenAI, Microsoft, and Perplexity for alleged copyright violations.' The other one says 'and my fiance works at Anthropic.'

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Oh my gosh!

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: That is amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So I mean, time limited, it's gonna be a time capsule, but I mean, made at the print shop in Brooklyn.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Brooklyn, one of a kind. Exists nowhere else on Earth.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: That's incredible.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And I... and I also...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Is this payback for when I gave you a hat at your wedding?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And I gave you one at your wedding, so I think we're even.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: That's true. We have a sort of a theme going on here.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Right. Well, and that's also our disclosure, which is that Kevin and I are buds and have known each other forever. So actually, Casey, you can come to me anytime. I know you guys like to rib and roast on the show, so you can come to me behind the scenes for any roastable Kevin material.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: My dream has been to get the New Yorker to investigate Kevin Roose. So you guys really could not have come along at a better time.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: We're on.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Don't tempt us.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I'm not picking up the phone.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Okay, let's talk about this big piece that you both just published in the New Yorker. The title of the piece is "Can Sam Altman be trusted?" Now, usually there's this sort of folk rule about headlines that end with question marks, which is that the answer is always no.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So, I want to put this question to you: can Sam Altman be trusted?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well, I think one important thing to note is the piece is really forensic and even. And actually to a point where I've been happy to see there's a range of reactions, right? There's people who have answered that question in a very severe way and looked at the fact pattern that is laid out here and the documentation that's laid out and said, you know, this is someone who poses an acute danger and should be kept away from an authority position.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And then there's people who, I mean, hilariously enough, my mother called me and she's like, "You know, I kind of like him."

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: (laughs)

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And so I think that is a true reflection of our intentions. In this case, as you might imagine, there was deep consultation with all of the subjects of the reporting to really understand their feelings. And anytime we thought there was a persuasive argument from Sam or anyone else that, you know, something shouldn't make it in or something would be sensationalist, we really carefully discussed that editorially.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So the result is very even and I would say on the question itself, what we lay out is something that is remarkable, I'd say, even against the backdrop of the culture of mistrust in Silicon Valley, where everybody understands and expects, right, that being a founder means telling different audiences different things at times to some extent, where everyone understands that the entire enterprise is building based on hype long before there is actual actionable deliverable product.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Even against that backdrop, there is an extraordinary preponderance of people who emerge from interactions with Sam Altman, including close years-long ones, with really active complaints and allegations that he lies repeatedly about things big and small.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, one of my favorites was when you quote him telling you that he wears a gray sweater every day to avoid...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: decision fatigue and then he shows up for his next interview in a green sweater.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: That felt like a really satisfying detail.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Thank you, I appreciated that.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I appreciated that eye for fashion that you so rarely get in these tech profiles.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And Drew was our fashionista in the writers' room.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Always. Always.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: But that's the kind of thing where, you know, we didn't want to make too much of that, right? Because it's like, oh, we caught you in this deep hypocrisy of, you know, choosing a green sweater. Like, and this is consistent with a lot of the things people said throughout the piece and throughout the career of Altman and OpenAI, is that there isn't this one smoking gun thing where he's like caught, you know, with his hand in the cookie jar. It's this sort of allegedly longer, more subtle accumulation of facts, which my kind of like glib and, you know, annoying way of describing it is like that the fabled memos and documents that were compiled that led to him being, you know, fired in 2023 and that have kind of dogged him throughout his career, they really shouldn't have been like a secret bullet-pointed list. They should have been a 16,000-word New Yorker piece, because when it—they only really make sense when you like lay them all out together in narrative form.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean you guys mention in your story that there have been sort of these rap sheets that have been circulated about Sam inside OpenAI and other parts of the AI industry for years. One of them was compiled by Dario Amodei when he worked at OpenAI under Sam Altman. One of them you said was maybe circulated by some allies of Elon Musk and people who are opposed to OpenAI. So give us some sort of behind-the-scenes details about what is being said by whom and how and to what end about Sam Altman in Silicon Valley.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well, it was really important to us to filter for the obvious competitive incentives out there. There are people who are massively incentivized to go after Sam Altman. And the reality is that there are very firmly evidence-based critiques, many of which are promulgated not just by the rivals, although they're certainly amplified by them happily, but also by more neutral figures and people who are just kind of technologists who aren't in the fight. And then there is the white-hot center of the rivalry, the stuff you mentioned that I think is in a very different category, which is, you know, Elon Musk and other direct competitors really amplifying everything they can come up with. And in some cases, we documented things that are inflated or trumped up or just seem to not be true. So Elon Musk in particular has intermediaries circulating some pretty spicy and pretty unsubstantiated material in Silicon Valley, and we talk about that.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I really appreciated that about the piece because

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: This has become like more salient over the past year as these rivalries heat up and you hear more and more of these scurrilous rumors and while I do think this winds up being a pretty damning portrait of Sam on the whole, you do also point out that in some very real ways he's the subject of legitimate smear campaigns.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, I think that's absolutely accurate. And we were trying not to go in, you know, with naivete of like can you believe business titans are being mean to each other? But like the level of this really does seem kind of shocking and unprecedented. And you know, it's kind of consistent with people who think of this as like whoever gets the ring first will control the world. Like it just seems like all bets are off and so as a reporter it's very challenging to be like, do you bring up the scurrilous rumors to knock them down? And so we had like months of conversations about how best to do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So there's been a lot of reporting on Sam Altman especially around the board coup a few years ago. Could you maybe give us like the two or three things that you think are new and important from your reporting that rise above the rest in terms of people's understanding of Sam Altman and OpenAI?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So I think there are things here that put to rest some of the long-standing rumors, right? I mean, you know, Altman has always said and Paul Graham at Y Combinator has always said he was not pushed out, he left of his own volition. It really seems from our reporting that that was not the case. Um, they have talked a lot about their um fundraising in the Gulf in the Middle East as innocuous, all businesses do this. It really seems from our reporting that the relationships that Sam has cultivated with some Emirati and Saudi royals is deeper than was previously realized. Um, Ronan, what am I missing? There are several things like this.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: We just didn't really know in full what was in those Ilya Sutskever memos. Um, we didn't really have the detailed multi-sourced heavily documented accounts of the individual proof points that were offered in those memos. We didn't have the contents of those Dario Amodei notes. Um, and we didn't have a lot of these people on the record yet. So I think actually in a way that was a disservice not only to Sam's critics but also to Sam himself, there was a bit of a veil of mystery. And, and that wasn't purely accidental. One of the things we document that's new here is as a condition of the exit of the board members who had moved against Sam that he wanted out, um, they insisted on an outside investigation. What happened there is in my view quite extraordinary, which is yes, at private companies, sometimes reports of this type when a law firm is brought in to restore legitimacy can be kept out of writing. Often it's to limit liability, um, and often legal experts say it's a bit of a red flag. This is a different kind of case. This isn't just any private company. This is a high-profile...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: scandal that engulfed Silicon Valley when Sam was fired.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And ostensibly at a non-private at a nonprofit.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: At a 501(c)(3) exactly. And so there were stakeholders, not just in the public, but you know, within this company. That would be the bare minimum threshold, right? Where senior executives thought, okay, we're going to get some kind of at least detailed summary of what this law firm investigation found when they invoke it to rubber-stamp Sam coming back. And instead what happened was an 800-word press release that said there had vaguely been a breakdown in trust and offered very few other details. And what we report in this piece for the first time is there wasn't a report. For years, people were like, where's the report? Where's the report? There wasn't a report because it was kept out of writing. And this is no longer just a speculative supposition. One of the two board members who Sam helped select, who oversaw this process, just explicitly says, well, you know, a written report was not needed is now their line on this.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I'm glad you brought it up. It was actually my favorite detail in the piece because it was something I'd been curious about forever. The thing that I found most interesting from the piece were the people who spoke on the record or at least, you know, gave you quotes. Some of them were unattributed about Sam who I think previously might have supported him or at least felt like there was no upside in sort of, you know, talking about him in a negative way in public. There was a Microsoft executive quoted in your piece as saying that there's a small but real chance he's eventually remembered as a Bernie Madoff or Sam Bankman-Fried level scammer. There's another unnamed board member who said, quote, "He's unconstrained by truth" and said that he has a, quote, "almost sociopathic lack of concern for the consequences that may come from deceiving someone." I haven't been on a lot of corporate boards, but I think that is something that is quite rare to hear a board member say about a CEO of a company. I'm just curious, like, when you were weighing these statements, did you feel like there are people who used to be fans of Sam who have soured on him or are these people who have really held a grudge against him for a long time?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: The thing that you point out about people changing their tune over time, I think is an integral part of what we document in the piece, which is, you know, the fact that Sam Altman comes up through this Y Combinator world is not incidental. The fact that he has an investment portfolio in, by his own estimation, you know, about 400 other tech companies. The fact that he has sat on everyone's board and everyone has sat on his board. I think our sort of line about this in the piece is like, we spoke to people who are Sam's friends, Sam's enemies, and given the mercenary nature of Silicon Valley, some people who have been both, right? So given that that's the landscape, you are gonna have people who change their tune as the wind blows different ways, and that's a lot of how Sam

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Altman's been able to weather a lot of this stuff in the past.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: One thing that results from that spread of opinions is to your question about evolving takes on Sam. There's definitely a class of nuts and bolts investors, prominent people in Silicon Valley who are really pragmatists, not just safetists, and who are growth and business oriented, who told us that at the time of Sam's firing, of the blip, they gave him the benefit of the doubt, and especially because of the factor we talked about before, where there just was a dearth of clear information.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: In that void, a lot of prominent people gave him the benefit of the doubt and saw only upside in bringing him back and removing the board that tried to fire him. There are a number of those prominent people in that category now who say, I don't know that I would have given him the benefit of the doubt if I knew everything then that I now know.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: It just strikes me though that everyone who digs into this winds up coming back with essentially the same story. You know what I mean? It's like there are not like 17 versions of Sam Altman out there like depending on which reporter calls which different source. I feel like we now sort of know like the broad outlines of this person's psychology.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I don't know. I want to challenge that. Like I do talk to people who are big fans of Sam, some of whom work for him, some of whom don't. Clearly this is a guy who has been able to at various points like lead very important technology projects and like rally people behind a vision. These people are not like mindless sheep, like they're critical and discerning and thoughtful people. So I don't want to like seem like I'm, you know, taking Sam's side on anything, but I just like I think that there are a lot of people with very strong feelings about Sam Altman, positive and negative. I think the positive side tends to be more like people defending him in private, and the public side tends to be more people criticizing him. But I don't know, I guess for Ronan and Andrew, like do you feel like there are vocal supporters who you came across in reporting the story who had sort of no direct employment relationship with OpenAI or Sam, or you know, weren't leading companies that he'd invested in or something who were like, yeah, this guy seems pretty good and smart and talented?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it was an 11-year-old who used ChatGPT to pass sixth grade.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Oh my god. No, no, for, there, there were legit defenders of, of Sam on a number of these fronts who we talked to for sure. I mean, I think a lot of this has to do with like what baseline expectation are you starting from. Like if you think of this as a business and you start from the premise that people who run giant successful businesses have to say a lot of different things to a lot of different people, like why is anyone even, why is this a story? I, I think though there's a kind of level setting here where one of the things you can do when you take a big sort of...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Putting everything in one place, narrative effort like this is you can start from the beginning and remember what the original pitch was.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And when you go back to what the original pitch was, the defense of, 'Why are you guys being so naive? This is a normal competitive business,' like, okay. So, when you pitched this as a non-profit safety-focused research lab that would aggressively comply with all regulation, like, were the people who believed that naive to believe it at the time? Right? So that's when the defenses start to feel a little more like pressured to me.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, also, like for what it's worth, you know, it's like, oh, you know, is it really a story that this guy's, you know, telling different things to so many different groups? It's like, that's not like really a story that gets told about Satya Nadella. It's not really a story that gets told about Sundar Pichai. It's not really a story that gets told about Tim Cook, right? Like there does seem to be something really unusual here. And my question for you guys, now that you've sort of spent some time immersed in this company, is what do you think it means for OpenAI?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, I mean luckily we have a really robust independent tech media to, you know, so I was going to tune into TBPN and see what their independent journalistic take on this would be.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Do you want to give listeners who may not be familiar with what you're talking about, some context here?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I think the day after our piece closed, Ronan, or something like, late last week, OpenAI acquired TBPN, which is this big sort of tech chat show. So that's one aspect of this answer, right? That as OpenAI expands and grows, they seem to be sort of buying up more of the press infrastructure to tell their own stories.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Relatedly by the way, a lot of announcements over there right concentrated around when they knew we were going to be running and right developed in the period where we were in these intensive conversations with them and many of them sort of pointed at the topics in the piece, you know, they announced this new safety fellowship that's very airy, they announced this new governance plan that's very sort of airy and ethereal, um, but are meant to I think, you know, occupy space in the conversation on the same topics. Sorry, go ahead, Ronan.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And look I mean everyone, Ronan, you should say more about this but everyone including Altman and the OpenAI execs we spoke to recognizes the economic pressures here. I mean, I think you guys were there when he said, 'Oh yeah, it's definitely a bubble and someone's going to lose a phenomenal amount of money,' right? So, it's not like even putting sort of the sci-fi Skynet stuff aside, you know, the economic pressures are unavoidable and...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: A lot of it has to do with this sort of pitchman rhetoric, the exact thing we're talking about, right? Because these things are contingent. It's not like oh will it be a bubble or not. It's like how hyped up will the cycle get is a byproduct of how people like Sam go around the world talking about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I want to ask sort of a basic question that I think people have probably raised with you, which is like, why does it matter who Sam Altman is? Like if what...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: What we're talking about is a technology that could have profound implications on national security, the economy, potentially the future of humanity. It doesn't seem obvious to a lot of people why it matters who is running these companies, because a very nice person who is very honest and very transparent in all their dealings could still release a rogue superintelligence that blows up the world, and a very, you know, manipulative person could release a very aligned model. And so what we should be paying attention to are the models themselves, not the people running the companies that make the models. I'm not saying I believe that, but I'm curious what do you make of that argument that we are focusing too much on the humans and not enough on the technology?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: We probably both have thoughts on this. I, I think I have two. The first of which is it's worth noting that while reasonable minds could perhaps differ on the question you just posed, the answer provided by Sam Altman and the founders of OpenAI was very clear, which is actually part of the way the entire enterprise was structured when it was founded as a nonprofit was they talked a lot about avoiding an AGI dictatorship. Um, they really believed that actually the person who gets there first and has the most power over this technology is pivotal. The individual integrity is formative to the way the technology goes and the way it's controlled and the way it's used. Um, the other thought that I have is in my mind, uh, you raise a valid point and more significant than any of this is the, the structures around these individuals. We have a technology emerging that could really affect us all in all of the existential ways you just mentioned, and we don't have the regulatory guardrails to keep an eye on these folks. We are completely seeding the power to these individual companies and their whims, uh, the mudfight between them, uh, the quality control that each of them uh has or lacks. I think that to me is the big question. And the integrity of an individual figures in that and it's important, but it reveals the weaknesses in the system. If you have someone who potentially lies all the time, uh, could in the eyes of many critics be a danger, the important thing is to have the structures that account for that.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: You, you, there's a great quote um that you guys have in the piece from uh one of his former coworkers who talk about how Sam now has this track record of setting up these elaborate guardrails to keep him in check and then skillfully navigating around them. And it made me wonder if you had seen this piece in The Information this week about tensions uh that are being reported between Sam and his Chief Financial Officer, Sarah Friar.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: ...Fryer. She's reportedly expressed doubts that OpenAI will be ready for an IPO this year and, according to the story, Sam has noticeably and awkwardly excluded her from some conversations related to the company's financial plans, kept her out of some key meetings. I read that and I was like, well, this is exactly what you guys are writing about in your piece, right? You sort of bring in somebody whose job it is to look over the finances of the entire company, get it ready for an IPO, but then for whatever reason, mmm, we're going to sort of exclude her from some meetings. So anyway, I just sort of feel like we really are seeing the exact pattern that you guys are writing about now repeating in real time.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and I mean just to, like, just to agree with all of this, I think the thing that Kevin's bringing up about given the power of this, why are we focusing on one personality? Like, I think that's very legitimate. I think that this is way beyond one person, this is way beyond one personality. It's not like the point of the piece is Sam shouldn't be AGI dictator, so Elon should or Demis should or whatever, right? It's to point out the fact that we're having a discussion about AGI dictators at all is insane. These guys know it's insane and yet this seems to be the race that they see themselves being in.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: When he was fired, he was brought back in part because I think no one could really imagine an OpenAI without Sam Altman. Do you think that's still the case?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I don't think it's unimaginable anymore. I think that part of reaching the scale that they've reached is that you can have a, you know, Steve Jobs figure be replaced by a Tim Cook figure. Right? It seems like it's inseparable from reaching this scale that that becomes at least a possibility in people's minds, right, Ryan? I mean, does that strike you that way?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. I think the landscape has changed substantially over the period of time we were reporting this story. The fact that gradually more and more people were talking openly about this critique is very telling. We report in the piece that there are periodic spasms of senior executives at OpenAI talking about succession again. Of course, naturally the company denies this, but also very interesting that in recent forms of that discussion, there has been talk about Fidji Simo being sort of the first potential successor candidate who could slot into any ideas of that type that circulate. Between our asking about that and the piece coming out, obviously Simo has now gone on leave for medical reasons. There's a lot of reshuffling. We see it in the Sarah Fryer case. I think you're right to link it to that quote that's in the article about constraints being sidelined. And yet, I think these doubts and questions persist and are now much more out in the open.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: On the leadership question, it just strikes me that for somebody, you know, who I assume wants to

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Stay CEO for a long time. It's interesting to me that he's hired so many former public company CEOs to be his top lieutenants, right? It's like he has the former CEO of Instacart there, he has the former CEO of Nextdoor there, he has the former CEO of Slack there. So, you know, that's... you're bringing a lot of really sort of sharp and pointed elbows into the room when you do something like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I'm trying to tell Sam that there's danger here.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Pro-tip... if you're listening Sam. The... you know, uh, there are people in this piece talking about earlier tracts of Sam Altman's career where they feel he was deliberately avoiding that. Actually part of what underpinned the terrible, terrible fumbling of the firing effort was a feeling that Sam had kind of stacked the board with, as one former member put it, uh, JV people, you know. Certainly, uh, if we're being more charitable than that, people who were unprepared for the ruthless corporate warfare that ensued. Uh, and, you know, I think one thing that has accompanied the emergence of this as a more openly discussed critique is that there's more people around this company, more stakeholders wanting, uh, you know, professionalizing influences in the mix.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I have to ask about one detail that I loved of the piece, which was that the first time that Sam Altman and Dario Amodei were scheduled to meet, um, they were going to meet at an Indian restaurant for dinner. This was back in I guess 2015 and, uh, and Sam texted him and said that his Uber had gotten in a crash and he was going to be 10 minutes late to dinner. Now you did not editorialize on that piece, but knowing you both, I'm sure that you went back through the... the Uber FOIA requests and found the... the logs of... of Sam Altman's Uber ride that night. Is it your belief that Sam Altman's Uber actually got in a crash?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I think we're just going to leave that as non-editorialized and let it stand right there by itself. I mean, I... we also, uh, I will say, uh, had this conversation and really liked just presenting that uninflected for consideration.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Okay, if you are the Uber driver who was driving Sam Altman to dinner with Dario Amodei and you are listening to this show, we do want to hear from you. We do want to hear your side. hardfork@nytimes.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: We will get to the bottom of this.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: We will.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well, it's a- it's a great piece. People should go read it. Uh, please do not investigate any other AI companies before my book comes out. Uh, it was- it was a very stressful week for me.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, why don't you guys take a nice long spring-summer break before you get back to it. Yeah, look into some politicians or Hollywood executives or something.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: We'll send you some names.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Luckily it takes us as long to write a piece as it takes you to write a book. So I think you're good- you'll beat us if we do anything else.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: There's two of you, should be faster.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Totally.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Ronan, Andrew, thanks so much for coming.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Thanks guys.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Thanks guys, your hats are in the mail.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Bless you.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Thank you for those, that's amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: That's very funny, I'm going to wear that.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well, Casey, it's been a pretty heavy show today, so we thought we wanted to end on a positive note with our segment called One Good Thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: One Good Thing, of course, our segment where we each talk about one thing that's been tickling our fancy lately. All right, Kevin, why don't you go first this time?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay, Casey, I am in love with this space mission. Yes, the NASA Artemis 2 mission. I have been totally and earnestly obsessed. My wife was like, 'You sure are talking about this space mission a lot.' I have been glued to this thing. And I have been filled with a kind of childlike glee and wonder that I did not know I still had the capacity to feel.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Now what exactly are they doing on this mission?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Orbiting the moon. They are going further than any humans have gone from Earth before. They have gone 252,756 miles from Earth. And if you're wondering how many miles is that? Well, the New York Times had a helpful comparison list.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And what did they find?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So you would need, you would need a chain of 2.37 billion of Nathan's famous hot dogs to cover the distance that this spacecraft has gone from Earth.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: That's great. Something we can all easily visualize. Thank you for that comparison.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Casey, I am learning things that I never expected to learn. I've been watching this with with my kid. I have become completely obsessed with like concepts and terms that I did not know a week ago, including corona structure, the terminator line, which I know what you're wondering, that sounds scary. It's actually the line that separates the sunlit side of the moon from the side that is dark.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I also learned that we don't call it the dark side of the moon. That's not the preferred astronomical term.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: What do we call it?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: The far side of the moon.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: The far side of the moon.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I am obsessed with all these astronauts that there are four of them up there. Victor, Christina, Jeremy, Reid. This is my Mount Rushmore. I love these people who I've never met. They are adorable. They are incredibly brave. And I think we should go to the moon every single year. I think we should give NASA whatever budget it needs to do because this has sort of reignited my faith in humanity.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. You know, I also saw somebody on social media was posting that because the mission specialist Christina Koch had communicated with Houston's Jenny Gibbons during the mission, this mission actually passed the Bechdel test, which you don't often see in a moon mission.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: ...on these missions. So I thought that was cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I also, somebody pointed out, they said, you know, the the coolest thing about going on one of these missions, Kevin, would be leaving Florida at 5,000 miles an hour. So that resonated with me as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay, you're more interested in the jokes. I am filled with childlike wonder over here and I just think this is the coolest thing imaginable.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: It is very cool. You know, recently I had an opportunity to go stargazing, I'm not sure if you've been stargazing recently. I was up on Mauna Kea on the the island of Hawaii.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Flex.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And uh and we had a really cool telescope there with our guide. And I got to stare at the face of the moon and it it inspired a childlike sense of wonder in me as well. But it did not make me want to go there because it looked quite bleak actually.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: You wouldn't go to the moon?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: No, there's no Wi-Fi.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay, Casey, what is your one good thing this week?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Today, Kevin, I want to talk about the only thing that can compete with the moon when it comes to inspiring childlike wonder in a person, and that is a weather app.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay, I'm listening.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So, recently I was reading about these entrepreneurs, Adam Grossman, Josh Rees, and Dan Abrutyn, and they are the team behind Acme Weather, which you probably have not heard of yet, but I bet you've heard of Dark Sky.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Dark Sky was by consensus the best weather app on iOS and while it reigned during the 2010s, and I'm using reigned in the sort of...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: The non-meteorological sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: The non-meteorological sense. It would tell you whenever it rained, and now I am using it in the meteorological sense. Very good app. RIP.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, this app was bought by Apple in 2020, which was like kind of a head-scratcher. Apple already had a weather app, it was fine. And then Apple sort of integrated some of its forecasts and some of its other features into its weather app and then shut Dark Sky down in 2022. And this made people really sad because I think a lot of us feel, myself included, like the Apple Weather app has never lived up to what Dark Sky was in its heyday.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: It's like a prediction market. It's like, you know, maybe it's going to rain.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Well, so these guys get back together and they say, 'Frick it, we're doing weather apps again,' and they make Acme Weather. And so you can download this now for iOS. It is apparently coming later to Android. And I know what you're thinking, Kevin, which is, 'What could you possibly build in 2024 in a weather app that could differentiate it from all the other weather apps that are already on the market, right?'

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Are you wondering this?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I am wondering this.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, let me tell you a few things. Number one, they don't just tell you the weather, they show you a range of possibilities in a line chart. So most of the time it'll be like, 'Yeah, it's going to be 63 degrees in San Francisco today,' but every once in a while there's a lot of volatility in all the...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: different signals that they use to predict the weather and then you say okay I don't actually know what I'm walking into today I better bring a couple of layers.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: This is the weather app for rationalists and other believers in Bayesian statistics.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Some of the other things that this app does, they will send you a push notification if they think there's going to be lightning in your neighborhood.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: They will also do that when they think a sunset is going to be beautiful in wherever you happen to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: They'll send you an umbrella reminder if it's going to precipitate in the next 12 hours and they'll send you a sunscreen alert when the UV index is high. But I've saving my last two favorites for the end. Number one, they will send you an alert when the Aurora Borealis may be visible where you are.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: That's beautiful.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I haven't gotten that notification yet, but I wake up every day hoping I'm going to get my Aurora Borealis notification.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Go to Scandinavia, I think.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Number two, and this is just in time for Pride. They will tell you when there is a rainbow in your neighborhood.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Wow. Are you kidding me? This is such a good idea for a weather app. Who does not want to be sitting at your wage slave job, you haven't been outside in like seven and a half hours, and then Acme Weather tells you, hey, guess what? There's a rainbow in your neighborhood. You're going to book it outdoors and you are going to behold the majesty of creation, Casey.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: How are they possibly collecting that data?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, interestingly, they're taking this Waze-like approach where they're inviting their community to submit reports and so if a bunch of people say hey, rainbow in my neighborhood, they're going to go out and send out a notification.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So now look, this app does cost $25 a year and I know, you know, probably most people out there are perfectly content with the free weather app on their phone. That is fine for you. But as somebody who loves cool things, new ideas, people having fun, I just wanted to shout out Acme Weather because I think it's a really cool thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Now what is the likelihood that this app will be purchased by Apple and then shut down?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I mean, if that happens, I hope these guys get paid again because somebody has to move the weather app industry forward and these are the folks who are doing it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I love that. Like Grandpa, how did you make your fortune? Well, I built 17 weather apps that were identical and then sold them all to Apple.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I just also think it's inspiring that at a time when some companies are like, we're going to make a system that is going to force the world to rewrite all software, there are other guys who are like, what if there's a rainbow in my neighborhood? I want to find out about that. And those are the people that I want to highlight on today's show, Kevin.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Okay, well download Acme Weather before the heat death of the universe renders weather irrelevant.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And tell us whether you liked it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: That was a good thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Thank you for alerting me to this wonderful rainbow detector.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, thank you for alerting me to the existence of the moon.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I know you weren't a big believer in the moon before, but hopefully I've convinced you today.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, somebody told me something about a soundstage and you know, maybe the landing was faked, so I've just been curious.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I think, you know, we're the only podcasters who actually believe in the moon. That's our competitive advantage.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Hard Fork, where we believe that people have been to the moon.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I'm Kevin Roose, a tech columnist for the New York Times.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And I'm Casey Newton from Platformer.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And this is Hard Fork.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: This week, we talk to Democratic Senator Michael Bennet of Colorado...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: ...about his plan to regulate social media.

highlights: The New Yorker profile on Sam Altman reveals an "extraordinary preponderance" of allegations that he repeatedly misleads colleagues and stakeholders, moving beyond Silicon Valley's typical culture of hype.

Reporting shows that the independent investigation into Altman’s 2023 firing from OpenAI never resulted in a written report, a move critics view as a strategic effort to avoid transparency and accountability.

Despite OpenAI’s original mission to prevent an "AGI dictatorship," current power structures allow individual tech leaders to control high-stakes technology without sufficient external regulatory guardrails.
New investigative details suggest Altman’s departure from Y Combinator was not voluntary and that his financial relationships with international royals are far deeper than previously acknowledged.
NASA's Artemis 2 mission is celebrated as a historic step in human exploration, marking the furthest distance humans have ever traveled from Earth as they prepare to orbit the moon.
summary:
This episode of *Hard Fork* features an in-depth discussion with *New Yorker* writers Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz regarding their expansive profile of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. The conversation centers on the pivotal question of whether Altman can be trusted, exploring a pattern of behavior where he allegedly tells different stories to different audiences to maintain control. The reporters highlight several key findings, including new evidence that Altman was pushed out of Y Combinator rather than leaving voluntarily, his deep financial ties to Middle Eastern royals, and the strategic decision by OpenAI’s board to forgo a written report after his brief ousting in 2023. The central theme is the tension between Altman’s visionary leadership and a "rap sheet" of allegations regarding his transparency, raising concerns about the lack of regulatory guardrails for the individuals leading the race toward Artificial General Intelligence.

The transcript also addresses the broader implications of Silicon Valley’s power dynamics, noting how Altman has historically sidelined dissenters and navigated around the very "guardrails" he helped establish. This is illustrated by recent reports of friction between Altman and OpenAI’s CFO, Sarah Friar, which the hosts suggest fits his established pattern of behavior. In contrast to these heavy themes of corporate intrigue, the episode concludes with the "One Good Thing" segment, which focuses on human wonder and ingenuity. Kevin Roose shares his excitement for NASA’s Artemis 2 mission to orbit the moon, while Casey Newton highlights "Acme Weather," a new app designed to alert users to beautiful natural phenomena like rainbows and the Aurora Borealis. These closing segments provide a hopeful counterpoint to the scrutiny of tech leadership, celebrating scientific exploration and the small joys of well-crafted technology.
description:
Today, we’re joined by Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz of The New Yorker to discuss their new blockbuster profile of Sam Altman. And afterwards, we look to the skies for the new edition of One Good Thing. Quick note: If you’re looking for our discussion on Anthropic’s new A.I. model Mythos, we published that one yesterday. https://youtu.be/oBZ5W758y4o 

00:00 Intro
02:01 Ronan Farrow, Andrew Marantz and the disclosure hats
03:42 The New Yorker profile. Can Sam Altman be trusted? 
09:12 The smear campaign(s) against Sam
09:49 OpenAI board coup 
13:05 “Bernie Madoff or a Sam Bankman-Fried-level scammer”
15:54 Sam Altman’s supporters
18:35 What does this mean for OpenAI? 
20:47 Why does this story actually matter?  
23:34 OpenAI IPO report from The Information
25:12 Can there be OpenAI without Sam Altman? 
28:16 First meeting between Dario Amodei and Sam Altman
30:15 One Good Thing — NASA Artemis II moon mission 
34:07 One Good Thing — Acme Weather app

Guest:
Ronan Farrow, investigative reporter and a contributing writer to The New Yorker.
Andrew Marantz, staff writer at The New Yorker.

Additional Reading:
Anthropic Claims Its New A.I. Model, Mythos, Is a Cybersecurity ‘Reckoning’
https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/07/technology/anthropic-claims-its-new-ai-model-mythos-is-a-cybersecurity-reckoning.html

Why Anthropic’s New Model Has Cybersecurity Experts Rattled
https://www.platformer.news/anthropic-mythos-cybersecurity-risk-experts/

Sam Altman May Control Our Future — Can He Be Trusted?
https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2026/04/13/sam-altman-may-control-our-future-can-he-be-trusted

Artemis II Moon Launch
https://www.nytimes.com/news-event/artemis-ii

Hard Fork is a weekly look into the future that's already here. Hosts Kevin Roose and Casey Newton explore stories from the bleeding edge of tech.
Casey’s publication, Platformer: https://www.platformer.news/

FOLLOW US ON SOCIAL

Kevin Roose - https://www.instagram.com/kevinroose/
Casey Newton - https://www.instagram.com/crumbler/

Twitter (X):
Kevin - https://x.com/kevinroose
Casey - https://x.com/CaseyNewton

Casey - https://www.threads.net/@crumbler

Subscribe to the podcast:
Apple - https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/hard-fork/id1528594034
Spotify - https://open.spotify.com/show/44fllCS2FTFr2x2kjP9xeT?si=86ba626802b849cd
Amazon - https://music.amazon.com/podcasts/7c7fe198-e6a8-41a8-b0fe-1d46b976dcd8/hard-fork
YouTube Music - https://music.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLB9gMmtMLXxsa8C0PzHFL2tJFh7FrKrYD&si=Fwb9Uwon1ZneNsBp
The New York Times - https://www.nytimes.com/column/hard-fork

“Hard Fork” is hosted by Kevin Roose and Casey Newton. Produced by Rachel Cohn and Whitney Jones. We’re edited by Vjeran Pavic. 

Engineering by Chris Wood and original music by Dan Powell, Marion Lozano, Sophia Lanman and Rowan Niemisto. Fact-checking by Caitlin Love. Our executive producer is Jen Poyant. Video production by Jake Nicol, Chris Schodt, and Sawyer Roque. Podcast Art Direction by Benjamin Wilkerson Tousley. Podcast Art & Animations by Julian Hespenheide. Additional Motion graphics by Phil Robibero. Thumbnails by Julia Moburg and Tara Godvin. Special thanks to Paula Szuchman, Pui-Wing Tam and Dahlia Haddad.
topics: A New Yorker profile investigating the character and trustworthiness of Sam Altman
The internal power dynamics and governance history of OpenAI
The launch and features of the Acme Weather app
